title: Mechouia salad
subtitle: Tunisian roasted pepper and tomato salad
excerpt: Red peppers, tomatoes, and garlic grilled directly over a flame until charred and collapsed, then peeled, chopped, and dressed with cumin, olive oil, and lemon. Piled onto a plate with tinned tuna and capers if you like. The Tunisian counterpart to Moroccan taktouka, sharper, smokier, and usually hotter.
sourceNotes: Mechouia (also spelled méchouia or meshwiya) means 'grilled' in Arabic. It is one of the essential Tunisian salads, served as a starter with brik or as part of a spread of salads. The combination of grilled red pepper and tomato is also found in Moroccan taktouka (which uses green pepper) and in many Mediterranean preparations. The Tunisian version is usually served with tuna and capers.

content.text: The char is the point. Roast the peppers and tomatoes directly over a gas flame or under the hottest possible grill until the skins are completely blackened. The tomatoes will split and collapse before the peppers are done, that is fine. The smoky, caramelised flavour of properly charred vegetables is what separates mechouia from a simple pepper and tomato salad.
The salad is chopped, not blended. After peeling, chop everything roughly with a knife on a board, incorporating the juices, until you have a rough, chunky salad. Do not use a food processor.

Roast the {{pepper-red}}, {{tomato}}, and unpeeled {{garlic}} cloves directly over a gas flame or under a very hot grill, turning with tongs, until completely charred on all sides. The tomatoes will take 8 to 10 minutes and the peppers 12 to 15 minutes. Transfer to a bowl, cover with cling film, and leave for 10 minutes. Peel the peppers and tomatoes (the skins will slip off easily), discarding the skins and pepper seeds. Squeeze the garlic from its skins. Chop everything together on a board into a rough salad, incorporating all the juices. Transfer to a serving plate. Dress with {{olive-oil}}, {{lemon-juice}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Scatter {{capers}} over the top if using. Serve with {{tuna-tinned}} flaked over the top if you like.

items.fix: After chopping, tilt the board and allow excess liquid to drain before dressing. Some tomato juice is essential to the flavour, but too much dilutes it
A direct flame or very hot grill (grill element at maximum, tray 5 cm below) is needed. Slow oven roasting softens the vegetables without charring the skin
items.cause: Peppers and tomatoes oven-roasted rather than grilled

items.fix: The unpeeled garlic cloves should be roasted until completely soft inside, the char on the outside mellows the flavour. If the garlic is still firm, continue roasting
items.cause: Garlic not properly charred and softened

content.text: Mechouia is on every Tunisian table as a starter, served with brik and crusty bread before the main course. With tuna and capers it becomes a substantial first course. The same flavour combination (roasted pepper, tomato, garlic) appears across the Mediterranean and North Africa, but the Tunisian version has a directness and depth from the char that distinguishes it. In Britain it makes a good summer salad, particularly alongside grilled lamb or as part of a Middle Eastern or North African spread.
